title: Causal inference and counterfactual prediction in machine learning for actionable healthcare
data_sources: Cooper et al. 1997 pneumonia hospital dataset (cited)
Cancer surveillance and clinical claims registries (cited)

data_scope.study_type: Review/Perspective — no new data collected or analyzed
data_scope.empirical_data_note: All empirical claims cite published primary studies; exact dataset sizes not stated in this paper

core_conclusion: Standard data-driven ML models trained on observational data cannot reliably support clinical interventions because they conflate conditional with interventional probabilities and carry confounding and selection biases. Trustworthy precision medicine requires causal approaches: the target trial framework (algorithmic RCT emulation), transportability (formal transfer of causal effects across populations), and prediction invariance (identifying causal models by cross-environment accuracy stability).
why_it_matters: Exposes a systemic conflation of prediction and intervention in health AI; provides three mathematically grounded safeguards; links confounding and collider bias to racial and demographic discrimination in clinical decisions; calls for education-level curriculum change; bridges ML and epidemiology for researchers without formal causal training.
why_nature: Paradigm-level Perspective critique challenging the foundational premises of ML-for-health; cross-disciplinary synthesis of do-calculus, DAGs, epidemiological study design, and deep learning; addresses AI ethics in high-stakes clinical contexts; timely corrective published as health AI commercialization was accelerating.
added_value_to_my_research: Causal target trial framework for estimating causal effect of spatial telehealth/facility accessibility on health outcomes from observational mobile-phone and EHR data
Spatial transportability layer to formally test whether 2SFCA/R2SFCA distance-decay functions calibrated in one city transfer to another geographic context
Prediction invariance as automated spatial audit criterion to verify whether ML spatial-prediction coefficients capture genuine causal place heterogeneity versus local confounders
Do-calculus formulation of facility-siting decisions P(outcome|do(site)) to produce causally valid effect estimates in healthcare and EV-charging equity optimization
Bias taxonomy mapping (confounding, collider, M-bias) onto demographic fairness audit for GeoAI health models incorporating race, income, and insurance-status variables

related.title: Deep learning of causal structures in high dimensions under data limitations
related.relation: automated causal structure learning to replace expert-specified DAGs in target trial pipeline

related.title: A causal perspective on dataset bias in machine learning for medical imaging
related.relation: spatial and dataset-shift extension of transportability across populations and imaging settings

related.title: Improving the quality of machine learning in health applications and clinical research
related.relation: complementary quality-improvement framework; operationalizes prediction invariance as a reporting standard

related.title: Active learning for optimal intervention design in causal models
related.relation: active causal intervention design directly extends the paper's do-calculus framework to facility siting

related.title: Algorithmic fairness and bias mitigation for clinical machine learning with deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: operationalizes the paper's bias-to-fairness link via deep RL for iterative bias mitigation

figures.caption: Conditional versus interventional probabilities — biomedical examples of observing P(D|R) vs. intervening P(D|do(R))
Examples of confounding bias and collider bias in DAG form
M-bias example: mother's diabetes history as collider in the education-diabetes causal pathway
Selection diagram for transportability: city A RCT causal effect transported to city B observational setting
